description: A condition is 'katheo' when its clinical course began recently, developed rapidly, or is expected to be short under the criteria being used. An acute injury may be mild or severe: 'pelaku' (severity) assesses degree of effect, while 'pukea' (danger) concerns possible harm. For persistence or return across the clinical source's duration, Phi uses 'nurawi' (chronic).
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release it into open central 'a'. Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th', pass air along it, and move into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Keep final true-mid rounded back 'o' in its own syllable across the 'e.o' hiatus by drawing the tongue back and rounding the lips.
usage_notes: Before a condition noun, 'katheo' describes its course; before 'nai' (be), it is the predicate. Noun use gives acute status. A chronic condition can have an acute episode, so context identifies which course the adjective describes and the time scale being used.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Time course and severity stay separate, so a quick onset cannot masquerade as evidence of danger or urgency.

semantic_domains.physical: recent onset, rapid development, or short duration in a bodily condition
semantic_domains.temporal: onset, development, and expected duration within a clinical course
